claim: Infection of human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 is most frequent in individuals of European origin.

result.claim: Infection of human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 is most frequent in individuals of European origin.
result.reasoning: The claim that infection of human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1) is most frequent in individuals of European origin is refuted by the evidence. A review paper titled "Epidemiological Aspects and World Distribution of HTLV-1 Infection" (Paper ID: W2008383971) explicitly states that HTLV-1 is not an ubiquitous virus and identifies the main highly endemic regions as the Southwestern part of Japan, sub-Saharan Africa, South America, the Caribbean area, and foci in the Middle East and Australo-Melanesia (Proposition ID: prop_29637, Chunk ID: chunk_4592). This paper, published in 2012, has a credibility rating of 2.8/5 and 1358 citations, indicating it is a well-regarded source in the field. A specific search for "HTLV-1 prevalence Europe" within this paper yielded no results, further supporting the conclusion that Europe is not a region of high HTLV-1 endemicity.

evidence.text: The estimated prevalence of vCJD infection was 237 per million (95% confidence interval 49–692 per million) in a retrospective study (Hilton et al. 2004).
